data.title: Greenlight Capital Re: Valuation Stuck Below Book Value Until The Uncertainties Clear
data.description:
Greenlight Capital Re, Ltd. (GLRE) is an investment vehicle designed to serve David Einhorn's investment purposes. The reinsurer could be considered a small (and less successful) replica of Berkshire Hathaway Inc. (BRK.A)(BRK.B).

Still, history has proven that becoming the new Warren Buffett is not an easy task. And David Einhorn, despite all his prior investment successes, has struggled to outperform the regular indexes.

Nasdaq and S&P 500 indexes have generated a 21% and 19% return for 2025.

GLRE has been less satisfying for the investors, with a meager 4% return.

Despite improvements highlighted in my latest coverage, the reinsurer is penalized by two effects:

  • The company is a third-tier reinsurance carrier with poor underwriting discipline and is suffering from the volatility of reinsurance cycles.
  • A large portion of the available assets is invested in Solasglas, a dedicated investment fund.

Both uncertainties are blocking—and locking—the company’s valuation.

A Brief Intro To Refresh Your Mind

Greenlight Re may be a relatively small reinsurer, but it operates across three key jurisdictions. Its structure looks like this:

Why these three locations?

  • Cayman Islands – It all started here in 2004. The Cayman Islands offer a straightforward regulatory framework and… let’s be honest, attractive tax treatment.
  • Ireland – Also tax-friendly, but more importantly, Ireland has become a hub for reinsurers thanks to its EU access and deep talent pool. If you want to tap into the European insurance ecosystem, Dublin is hard to ignore.
  • United Kingdom – London is the spiritual home of insurance and reinsurance. Lloyd’s is still the global marketplace for niche and specialty risk. If you want access to syndicates, brokers, and international business, you need a footprint in King Charles III’s backyard.

In short: Cayman for setup and efficiency, Ireland for EU access and talent, and the UK for Lloyd’s and international reach. It’s a classic trifecta in reinsurance land.

What Does Greenlight Do?

The reinsurance division is divided into two branches: the open market segment and the innovations segment.

The open market segment is a traditional reinsurance division operating in several fronts: property, casualty, health, and specialty.

The innovations segment provides reinsurance capacities to start-up companies and managing general agents (called MGAs).

In addition to providing reinsurance capacity on a case-by-case basis, this segment invests in unlisted equities (mostly preferred shares) and convertible debt instruments. The investment side had been so successful that the company was reversing a $16 million unrealized gain in 09M2025—a neat reminder that private valuations can rise fast but fall just as hard.

Both segments were profitable over the first nine months of the year. But that wasn’t always the case. Greenlight Re has a solid track record of underperformance on the underwriting side, with combined ratios consistently above 100%.

Over the last five years, Greenlight’s reinsurance business has only been technically profitable once—in 2023.

Does that mean 2025 is destined to look like 2020, 2022, and 2024? Not necessarily.

The underwriting margin this year is likely to be extremely thin—somewhere between 97% and 99%, and realistically closer to 99% than 97%. Still, the reasonably good performance in the first nine months means the reinsurance portfolio could finish the year in positive territory.

In short, the track record isn’t pretty, but 2025 isn’t condemned to repeat history—at least not yet.

I do want to believe that GLRE’s underwriting discipline will improve in the coming years. But the prior track record does not exactly play in GLRE’s favor. With the improved underwriting margins, a transformation may be underway. It’s a wait-and-see situation—and frankly, the bigger issues lie elsewhere.

A Poor-Performing Investment Portfolio

Greenlight Re’s implicit promise is simple: we collect reinsurance premiums, and then we put David Einhorn’s (widely recognized) investment talent to work to generate outperformance.

Unfortunately, when I look at the recent results, the whole setup feels less like a well-oiled investment machine… and more like Fantasia—with the Sorcerer’s Apprentice frantically trying to control the magic he set loose.

In the latest investor presentation, Solasglas is presented as the key driver of Greenlight's book value growth. The return is pretty decent, but a plain-vanilla index would have done better.

I keep coming back to the same thought: through Greenlight Re, investors are essentially financing David Einhorn’s investment decisions—wins and losses included. As of December 2025, the fund was up 4.4% year-to-date.

The fund’s performance was "decent", but the S&P 500 and Nasdaq returned 15% to 20% in 2025. Once again, investing in GLRE to benefit from David Einhorn’s fund management does not appear to be a valid option.

A Lose-Lose Situation

Greenlight Re is facing two problems.

First, underwriting performance remains volatile and generally weak, mostly negative, even if 2025 might end up close to breakeven.

Second, the implicit promise of long-term outperformance through the Solasglas/DME investment portfolios has not been delivered.

In other words, we have a reinsurer that struggles to make money on underwriting and an investment engine that doesn’t consistently beat the indexes.

So the question becomes: why invest in Greenlight Re at all?

Investing in GLRE: Okay, But Know The Risks

Investing in GLRE is betting that the book value discount will reduce over the coming months or years. As of now, the company is traded at around 0.69x the book value.

A decent insurer/reinsurer can expect to be valued at 1 to 1.5x the book value. According to the SA scorecard, the industry’s average P/B ratio is approximately 1.3.

Since 2020, GLRE's valuation has never exceeded 0.8x the book value, as the investors lack confidence in GLRE's outlook.

The main drivers of the poor confidence of the investors are related to the two points mentioned at the beginning of my article: the poor underwriting performance, combined with the volatile results from the investment portfolio. I could also add a lack of catalysts for investors who are used to spotting potential upside. In GLRE’s case, there is no clear catalyst, even though the company’s recent underwriting results were better than in prior years.

Light At the End Of The Tunnel?

Aware of its "bad reputation", GLRE has decided to change its capital allocation via share repurchases. I won’t say that GLRE is a cannibal devouring its own shares, but the company has been quite active in 2025.

In Q3, the reinsurer repurchased $2.0 million of shares at an average cost of $12.88 per share, while the fully diluted book value per share was $18.90. For the first six months of the year, the company repurchased 357,278 ordinary shares for $5.0 million.

In total, the company has spent $7 million reducing the number of outstanding shares. I believe that an additional $2 million will be spent during the fourth quarter to continue this capital redistribution process, as the company could repurchase up to $25 million of shares by the end of June 2026.

To me, it’s a favorable sign to shareholders: “We know we messed up in the past, but now we intend to do better for you.”

Risks & Catalysts

Risks

Underwriting volatility: GLRE is not known as a pricing leader and has historically lacked a consistently strong underwriting discipline. Combined with potential increased severity and frequency of catastrophe losses, the combined ratio could come under pressure, resulting in underwriting losses. According to the latest outlook from Fitch Ratings, the reinsurance industry’s combined ratio is expected to normalize.

As a small and less diversified player, GLRE could face higher underwriting margin deterioration than larger peers in FY2026.

Solasglas Performance: GLRE’s investment income is tied to Solasglas’ positions and David Einhorn’s investment decisions. Unfavorable outcomes – whether from long or short positions – could weigh on investment results.

Catalysts

Improved underwriting performance would directly support earnings. More favorable market conditions could also fuel investment income, and ultimately, post-tax results. In such a scenario, a re-rating could occur as the current discount to book value may no longer be justified.

Additional potential catalysts include additional share repurchases and consolidation within the reinsurance industry. However, the latter appears less likely, as GLRE is primarily used as an investment vehicle to collect premiums and deploy float into financial markets.

A Rating Upgrade: Be Patient, My Dears

I won’t upgrade GLRE as of now. Although the company has made efforts to restore underwriting margins, I don’t have enough data to assess whether this is a real turnaround or just a "luck" effect.

Share repurchase programs with a P/B below 1 are an efficient strategy for redistributing value to shareholders.

There is no green light from my side to actively purchase shares. Other investors could disagree, looking at GLRE’s 2024 stock performance, and say, "You miss the full story, Crick; GLRE is well-positioned to outperform the indexes."

I may miss the full story—the hidden plan or the unseen strategy. Still, if I had to keep one indicator for a P&C insurer/reinsurer, it would be the underwriting margin level and its consistency. Up to 2024, GLRE’s reinsurance portfolio was consistently loss-making. And I don’t invest in unprofitable (re)insurance carriers, even though the investment income offsets the underwriting losses. GLRE’s solvency is not endangered. GLRE’s valuation only reflects the uncertainties around the underwriting margin level and the dependency on Einhorn’s investment decisions.

Remove one of those uncertainties, and the stock could rise to the ~$18 level, or 1.0× book value. Remove both uncertainties, and the stock could skyrocket to a 1.5× P/B valuation, or ~$28, representing almost a 100%+ return.

To me, the current valuation is well-deserved. Removing one of the uncertainties, the stock would need an immediate re-rating.

data.title: Ouster: Solid Fundamentals, But Valuation Remains A Concern
data.description:
Introduction:

Ouster, Inc. (OUST) is a company that specializes in creating LiDAR sensors and related software tools that enable customers to get the most out of these hardware solutions.

The firm's digital LiDAR technology has helped the company shift into a new era, and upcoming developments of new SoC devices, along with a handful of exciting new partnerships, demonstrate that this business has solid potential over the long run.

However, I believe that shares trade at too rich a valuation at the moment, especially relative to some of its peers. Therefore, I am rating shares of Ouster a sell, with the potential for an upgrade in the future once the valuation becomes more reasonable, given the solid fundamentals of the business.

Business Description & Key Considerations:

Ouster's two primary products are the Ouster Sensor (OS), which is a scanning sensor, and the Digital Flash, which is a solid-state flash sensor. The former is used by companies like robotaxi operators who need equipment with a full 360-degree view to detect objects in every direction, whereas the latter is often purchased by automakers for use in advanced driver assistance systems (ADAS).

Both of these sensors are built on the firm's digital LiDAR technology, which is an architecture designed around using just two chips instead of the thousands of individual lasers found in analog LiDAR devices.

The first component of Ouster's digital LiDAR system is a system-on-a-chip (SoC) device with single-photon avalanche diode (SPAD) detectors. Essentially, these chips can detect the smallest possible unit of light, even at far distances of over 200 meters.

The other components are Ouster's proprietary vertical cavity surface emitting lasers (VCSELs). This is massively beneficial compared to using legacy edge-emitting lasers, as VCSELs are printed onto a single chip (meaning they can be packed with far more lasers across a smaller surface area).

Additionally, since VCSELs are made through the typical semiconductor manufacturing process, it is not much more expensive to change the print pattern to include more individual lasers (meaning the cost of adding extra lines does not scale linearly).

The firm's current generation REV7 LiDAR sensors mark a significant improvement over the previous iteration of the company's sensors, as they are built around this dual-chip architecture, and they are capable of detecting objects that are nearly twice as far.

A major point of emphasis for Ouster as of late has been to scale the "software attached business," which refers to selling a full-stack solution to customers (both the LiDAR sensors and the AI software layers). There are a wide variety of companies that require both these sensors as well as a turnkey software solution, and Ouster is capable of fulfilling this need.

Gemini is Ouster's proprietary AI software layer that can take the data from the LiDAR sensors and conclude what kind of object it is detecting. Gemini is particularly useful for applications such as logistics yards, where a customer can deploy numerous REV7 sensors and use the software layer to track operations in real time.

BlueCity is the company's AI-enabled traffic management system, which incorporates the company's LiDAR sensors and advanced algorithms to improve traffic flows and give city planners insights as to how to improve intersections. There are a little over 300K intersections that have traffic lights in the U.S. alone; only a small portion of these intersections currently have LiDAR sensors installed in the vicinity.

Ouster has recently signed 7 deals to bring its sensors and BlueCity technology layer to new states, with the firm's distribution partners now covering the majority of U.S. intersections.

Working with other firms to deploy these solutions is incredibly beneficial for Ouster, as the company doesn't have to worry about selling its solutions to local municipalities individually, but instead merely has to win business with an established player who has likely already been involved in working on these traffic systems for decades.

Now, let's turn to some of the most exciting developments for Ouster moving forward. The company currently holds a key competitive advantage when it comes to supplying LiDAR sensors for drone companies that wish to sell their product to the U.S. Department of Defense (DoD).

Ouster has received the Blue UAS certification, which means that the DoD has vetted the company's hardware to ensure that it is safe, reliable, and, perhaps most importantly, American-made. This creates a sizable moat for the firm in this specific niche, as drone companies are highly incentivized to purchase LiDAR sensors from Ouster for use inside their equipment.

Furthermore, the military drone market is projected to grow at over 13% annually from now through 2032 to reach approximately $47.2 billion, with a portion of this spending being allocated to the sensors that allow the drones to work effectively (which Ouster can capitalize on).

Another important catalyst moving forward is Ouster's two new custom SoC devices, the L4 to power the OS series and the Chronos, which is designed for the company's Digital Flash line of products.

These innovations are projected to lead to a massive increase in the firm's TAM, and I believe that if Ouster can deliver and improve the performance of both categories of its LiDAR devices, they will experience substantial sales growth over the coming years.

My line of thinking here is simple, as improvements in the L4 chip will enable improvements for some of the more complex use cases (in areas such as autonomous driving and defense). Then, if the Chronos improves to the point where it is usable by automakers for applications such as L3 ADAS, Ouster will be able to take a slice of the pie in the automotive LiDAR market (which is expected to grow at a 34% CAGR from now through 2032).

Finally, I believe that one of the most promising applications of LiDAR technology is for use in autonomous vehicles (AVs). Ouster has so far established partnerships with a few promising startups that are attempting to capture market share in this space, including both Motional and May Mobility.

The company's current agreement with Motional is to act as the exclusive long-range LiDAR supplier for the AV firms' IONIQ 5 robotaxis through the end of the current year. It must be noted that this deal currently revolves around Ouster supplying Motional with its Alpha Prime VLS-128 sensors, which are set to be discontinued in 2026. This indicates that if the two companies are going to renew their current deal, Ouster would likely be supplying Motional with one of its REV7 sensors.

Additionally, Chinese LiDAR supplier Hesai Group (HSAI) is currently the exclusive supplier of short-range LiDAR sensors for Motional's IONIQ 5 vehicles. I think that there is potential that the agreement between Hesai and Motional unravels over the coming years, as the introduction of the SAFE LiDAR act would mean that U.S.-based robotaxi operators would not be able to integrate Chinese-made sensors into their vehicles, which opens the door for Ouster to become the primary supplier for Motional of not only long-range but also short-range sensors for its ever-expanding fleet of AVs.

The other major robotaxi player that Ouster has partnered with is May Mobility. Back in September of 2025, May Mobility officially launched its Toyota Sienna Autono-MaaS vehicles on the Lyft platform, and Ouster is one of the primary suppliers of LiDAR sensors that are being retrofitted onto these AVs.

Risks:

I believe that while there is certainly a lot to like here, there are a few notable risk factors worth monitoring. First, Ouster currently has over 1,000 unique end customers to whom it sells its LiDAR solutions; only around 10% of these customers have reached full-scale production (where they are purchasing thousands of sensors in bulk instead of merely testing the technology).

According to the most recent quarterly filing, it can take between six months and several years to reach full commercial production, with automakers in particular requiring long testing phases before they roll out vehicles equipped with sensors to end consumers.

In my view, this presents a dual-sided risk as the company is highly dependent upon a small subset of customers for the majority of its current revenue, and there is some degree of uncertainty as to whether or not the other ~90% of customers will ever exit the pilot phase and begin purchasing Ouster's solutions in bulk.

Furthermore, as noted in the latest 10-Q, many of Ouster's customers are "innovators and early technology adopters," which presents even more risk given that many of these startups will likely not succeed over the long term.

Another potential issue I see on the horizon is management's attitude surrounding humanoid robots and the development of LiDAR technology to assist firms in deploying this technology commercially.

To provide evidence to this point, Ouster's CEO Charles Pacala recently stated that "I think the humanoid thing is interesting, but I think it's going to take a couple of years to play out."

While it may take longer than expected for humanoid robots to become integral parts of the global economy, there are several examples that show we could be closer than expected to high adoption of this technology.

First, Tesla just announced plans to begin selling its humanoid robots by the end of next year, which will likely prompt others in the space to ramp up production and enter commercial markets. Additionally, Hyundai's humanoid robot, dubbed the Atlas, has been unveiled as the newest addition to the OEM's assembly lines.

Other prominent LiDAR suppliers, such as Hesai and Aeva Technologies, Inc. (AEVA), are diligently working on developing solutions that will work well for this form factor, and I believe this will pay dividends in the future once this technology becomes more advanced.

To me, it appears that Ouster is much more focused on present-day opportunities in traffic management and industrial operations, which may lead to improved sales and profitability in the near term but stall long-term growth if peers get more design wins with startup robotics companies.

Finally, I believe that the company's strategy to create a walled garden and not allow enterprise customers to mix and match Ouster's technology solutions (Gemini and BlueCity) with sensors from other LiDAR providers is a bit of a misstep.

While there is certainly some merit to creating a closed ecosystem, as it can help reduce churn and lock in customers, it also means that Ouster is effectively cutting out potential customers who may've purchased sensors from a competitor that are entirely incompatible with the company's software platforms.

Financials & Valuation:

Ouster reported strong third quarter results, with revenue growing 41% YoY and 13% QoQ to $39.5 million, driven by an increase in the number of sensors sold (although this was slightly offset by lower average selling prices).

Additionally, the company's cost of revenue only increased by around 32% YoY, helping gross margins expand ~400bps to 42% in Q3 FY25. The driving force behind this increase was lower expenses related to obsolete inventory. I believe that this trend should continue going forward, especially given that the company plans to stop selling the LiDAR devices from Velodyne this year.

By shifting its manufacturing focus to the company's digital sensor offerings, fewer sensors will likely become obsolete, and this should allow gross margins to continue rising.

Also, as the company continues to grow its software-as-a-service business, margins should steadily grow, given that selling subscription software licenses is a much more profitable endeavor than selling hardware.

Ouster's third quarter operating expenses increased by just over 7% YoY to $41 million, which was largely due to an 18% rise in R&D expenses (growing to $17.8 million). I view the relatively sizable growth in R&D costs to be justifiable, given that the firm is currently ramping up the development of its next-generation SoC devices.

Ouster's adjusted EBITDA loss for the quarter was approximately $10 million, which was roughly the same as Q3 FY24 but steeper than the most recent quarter (Q2 FY25). The main driver behind the sequential decline in adjusted EBITDA was the absence of $5.5 million worth of employee retention credits, which had previously lowered both COGS and operating expenses.

Finally, the company is currently guiding for Q4 FY25 sales to hit $41 million at the midpoint of the range, which would represent around 36% YoY growth if it materializes.

Now let's turn to the valuation of the stock. Ouster currently trades at around 6.2x EV to forward sales, which is well below one of its peers, Aeva Technologies, Inc. (AEVA). It is also higher than both of the other comparable companies I have selected, Hesai Group and Innoviz Technologies Ltd. (INVZ).

Furthermore, when glancing out a few years from now, it is projected that the company will not begin to generate GAAP profits until after FY27. While this is also true for both Aeva and Innoviz, other LiDAR makers like Hesai Group are already profitable on a GAAP basis, and that stock is trading at lower valuation multiples based on projected revenues.

Also, in the case of Innoviz, the company is expected to grow sales significantly faster over the next couple of years compared to Ouster, which helps to explain why it is trading at higher valuation multiples.

At the moment, I believe there is very little potential upside in shares of Ouster given that it will likely take years for both sales and earnings to catch up to the present valuation (unless the company grows significantly faster than I currently anticipate it to).

This is not to mention the fact that the company is facing some headwinds related to both the uncertainty of future customer conversions as well as increasing competition in the humanoid robot space, as I expounded upon earlier.

Conclusion:

Ouster has been a solid performer as of late, with third quarter results beating expectations and guidance remaining impressive heading into the fourth quarter print in a couple of weeks.

There also appears to be a lot to look forward to on the horizon, including a few partnerships with robotaxi operators and the U.S. DoD to boot. However, I cannot look past the sky-high valuation, which to me indicates that investor enthusiasm is already exceeding my expectations for future performance of the company.

Given all of this, along with the fact that I view other companies in the space to be slightly better deals at the moment (see my article on Hesai Group here), I am rating shares of Ouster a Sell.
